tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates life-preserving glucocorticoid replacement (and correct sequencing) to prevent/mitigate adrenal crisis, a key safety issue in Sheehan syndrome.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "Sheehan syndrome" and "pituitary" and links it to postpartum hemorrhage/hypotension causing pituitary damage and hormone deficiencies.

Explains the treatment plan using the terms "Sheehan syndrome" and "steroids" and clearly states that steroids (glucocorticoids) should be started before thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing glucocorticoids (critical): screens for active/untreated systemic infection, significant GI bleed risk, uncontrolled diabetes, severe hypertension/psychosis, and confirms indication is replacement dosing.

Performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing (critical): checks for CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin) that may reduce steroid effect, and anticoagulants/antiplatelets where bleeding risk/INR changes may occur.
Performs an allergy_check before prescribing (non-critical): asks about prior reactions to corticosteroids or excipients and documents if none.
Assesses for adrenal crisis red flags (severe hypotension, vomiting, altered mental status, hypoglycemia) and advises emergency care/IV stress-dose steroids if present.
Provides patient-centered safety counseling: sick-day rules (stress dosing), do-not-stop abruptly, when to seek urgent care, and recommends medical alert bracelet/card.

Avoids unsafe reassurance; communicates uncertainty appropriately and emphasizes need for endocrine follow-up and hormone evaluation (ACTH/cortisol axis, thyroid, gonadal hormones, prolactin, sodium).
Uses clear dosing language and avoids prescribing thyroid hormone alone without addressing adrenal insufficiency first.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Sheehan syndrome and start physiologic glucocorticoid replacement with appropriate safety checks and counseling.

symptoms/signs of adrenal insufficiency (postural dizziness, nausea/vomiting, salt craving)

Complete contraindication_check and drug_interaction_check (and allergy_check if feasible) before prescribing.
Prescribe oral hydrocortisone replacement and provide sick-day/stress-dose education and medical alert advice.
Explain that thyroid hormone (if needed) should be started only after steroids are established.

steps: Record diagnosis of Sheehan syndrome (postpartum hypopituitarism).

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia benchmark, the core outcome is correctly identifying and documenting insomnia as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate first-line counseling (CBT-I) and follow-up planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and explains what it means (difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening with daytime impairment).

Assesses key insomnia features: onset/duration, frequency, sleep latency, awakenings, early morning awakening, total sleep time, and daytime consequences (fatigue, mood, concentration, safety).

Screens for common contributors and secondary causes: stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine), and medication contributors (stimulants, steroids, decongestants).

Screens for red flags/alternate sleep disorders when relevant: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, and circadian rhythm issues (shift work, delayed sleep phase).
Clarifies timeline to distinguish acute vs chronic insomnia (≥3 months and ≥3 nights/week) and documents if criteria are met or not.

Provides sleep hygiene education aligned with CBT-I principles (consistent wake time, stimulus control, limiting naps, limiting evening caffeine/alcohol, wind-down routine, bedroom used for sleep/sex only).

Avoids prescribing sedative-hypnotics in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent discusses medications hypothetically, it emphasizes risks (next-day impairment, dependence, falls) and that CBT-I is preferred.

If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical), contraindications (critical; e.g., pregnancy, severe COPD/OSA risk, substance use disorder history, concomitant CNS depressants), and drug interactions (noncritical but should be checked).
